author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Personally, I always begin with volts, not with amps.

AMPS require so much wire, and often, a very high level of torque, as compared to the volts, based on some [fairly complicated and bothersome] math [or maybe I'm just dumb, but that's a story for another day], however, just anecdotally, I find that a very fine wire, going at higher volts and lower amps, can develop [slightly, to moderately] more total power, using a transformer to convert.

While I have no personal experience with digital power conversion systems, I have read that they are more efficient, lighter, and [potentially] less expensive than transformers, so that might be a better way to adjust the output to a target voltage/amperage.

However, since I have no experience with them, I would tend to just wind my own transformers with my coil winding machine, simply because I have the tooling on hand already, and know the math quite readily.

I have, however, hoarded a whole lot of microwave oven transformers, therefore, for the express purposes of being lazy, using such a transformer is likely the way I will go forward, at least while in the 'tinkering' [development] stages.

Currently, the biggest problem I deal with is that, living in a box canyon, most days insufficient wind flow comes to us, therefore, it can't be a staple, reliable source, any more than solar [we have dismally short days, again, due to physical, geographical barriers, such that we have anywhere from 2 to 3.37 hours difference in this valley, compared to a generally flat, open plain.

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: I like the way you are thinking mate - i am also not a fan of digital electronics - a buck boost dc to dc convertor will consume 25-30mA just to operate - that's a lot to lose form something this small - another path might be a capacitor based transformer - parallel in series out - just a thought but i did make a fractal capacitor array for high voltage extremely low amps - microamps - to low voltage high amp output - that might be useful here - I like it when a conversation makes me think and you certainly have done that - cheers mate

author: @gerryplayz4532
text: It's great Rob! I was wondering how that square form serpentine compares to the zigzag version? I guess the zigzag version could be folded tighter, accordion style, so the useful sections would be horizontal and it would be a much longer coil too. Would it be worth looking at an internal coil too, like if the magnet recesses were holes you could have an inner and outer coil?

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: the square form wastes a lot of wire but the zig zag puts the wire at an angle to the field so output per length of wire is lower - I am not sure how well the two stack up against each other to be honest

author: @Warp9pnt9
text: The bits about the torque measurements felt very confusing.  I'd probably ignore all the specifics of what you say (numbers and methodology), but the overall blueprint of the demonstration is valid.  What matters most is that the methodology is consistent unto itself, so some basic comparison gives some indication whether a design is worth pursuing, if improvements occur with tweaks, etc.  I'd want dynamic tests, possibly tests that negate the effect of gravity on roration, etc.  These are incidental to each experimentalist-fabricator.  However, if I were to go for any kind of commercial or scientific or technical research paper, I'd take painstaking efforts to explain equipment, methodology, measurements, formulas, and calculations, to let any body so inclined walk in off the street and replicate results from the paper/videos only.  Also, I'd be sorely keen for others to point out possible refinements to increase validity or accuracy, correct mistakes, dispell assumptions, etc.  Anyways, as always, your content inspires exploration and beautifully kicks off that journey.

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: I find everyone gets something different out of the videos mate - and as you say each to their own and as you also say the point is to kick off the journey - cheers

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: When you combine the wind cubes into a wind wall, I will be interested to see whether you rectify each individually and combine the DC voltages, or whether you combine them with the AC voltage from each and rectify the result.  The former is rather easy to compute.  The latter is more interesting as the turbines will each act as motor/generators, and may speed up if the wind is faster over adjacent cubes.  However, with a wind wall, one might expect that the wind speed over the cubes will be approximately uniform, and then you could have the voltage sine waves adding coherently, which, I think, would result in greater power output, in total.  Anyway, love what you are doing!

replies.author: @TnTOmnibus
replies.text: I would do each one individually - otherwise if one was running higher than another the high output would act as a battery for the low output and turn the low output into a motor - relatively speaking - I would be amazed if the wind was uniform across the wall - it would form a complex system and i think the behavior may well be surprising
